# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> All questions below are modelled strictly on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern for PEPOT525. Marks are split as: **Part A (3 marks each)** for short-answer items, and **Part B (14 marks each)** for full descriptive problems with internal choice. Bloom's levels are tagged.

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "degree of swelling" of a polymer gel and list the two primary thermodynamic contributions that oppose each other to set the equilibrium swelling.** *(Bloom Level: Remember / Understand — CO2)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **degree of swelling**, $Q$, of a polymer gel is defined as the mass of solvent imbibed per unit mass of dry crosslinked polymer:

$$Q = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_d}$$

where $m_s$ is the mass of the fully swollen gel at equilibrium and $m_d$ is the mass of the dry (xerogel) sample (1 mark).

The two opposing thermodynamic contributions are: (1) the **mixing (osmotic) free energy contribution** $\Delta G_{mix}$, which arises from the thermodynamic compatibility of the solvent with the polymer chains and provides the *driving force* for swelling; and (2) the **elastic retractive free energy contribution** $\Delta G_{elastic}$, which originates from the configurational entropy loss of the network chains as they are stretched between crosslinks during swelling and provides the *resistance* to swelling (2 marks).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Flory–Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$, and what does its numerical value qualitatively indicate about polymer–solvent compatibility?** *(Bloom Level: Understand — CO2)*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** $\chi$ is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies the energetic interaction between a polymer and a solvent in the Flory–Huggins lattice theory of polymer solutions (1 mark).

It is related to the Hildebrand solubility parameters by $\chi \approx 0.341 + (V_1/RT)(\delta_p - \delta_s)^2$ (1 mark).

- If $\chi < 0.5$, the polymer–solvent interactions are favourable (good solvent), the gel swells strongly, and $Q$ is large.
- If $\chi > 0.5$, the polymer–solvent interactions are unfavourable (poor solvent), the gel collapses and $Q$ is small (1 mark).

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question B — Choice A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the necessary assumptions, derive the Flory–Rehner equilibrium swelling equation for an affine polymer network swollen in a pure solvent. Clearly define every symbol that appears in the final result.

**(b) [7 marks]** A dry polyacrylamide hydrogel sample of mass 0.250 g swells to a final mass of 3.85 g when immersed in water at 25 °C. Using $\rho_p = 1.30~\text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_s = 1.00~\text{g/cm}^3$ and the literature value $\chi = 0.48$, calculate: (i) the degree of swelling $Q$, (ii) the equilibrium polymer volume fraction $v_{2s}$, and (iii) the average molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$ using the affine Flory–Rehner equation. Take $V_1 = 18.04~\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice A)

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**(1 mark)** *Statement of the thermodynamic equilibrium criterion.* At swelling equilibrium, the chemical potential of the solvent inside the gel equals that in the pure solvent reservoir:

$$\mu_1^{gel} = \mu_1^{pure} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \pi_{mix} + \pi_{elastic} = 0$$

**(2 marks)** *Mixing (osmotic) pressure from Flory–Huggins theory.* The free energy of mixing for a polymer of volume fraction $v_2$ in a solvent of molar volume $V_1$ is

$$\frac{\Delta G_{mix}}{RT} = n_1 \ln(1 - v_2) + N_2 \ln v_2 + n_1 \chi v_2$$

Differentiating with respect to solvent moles $n_1$ (with $V = n_1 V_1 + V_0$ and $\partial V/\partial n_1 = V_1$):

$$\pi_{mix} = -\frac{RT}{V_1}\left[\ln(1 - v_2) + v_2 + \chi v_2^{\,2}\right]$$

**(2 marks)** *Elastic pressure for an affine network.* The relative volume on swelling is $V/V_0 = 1/v_2$, hence the isotropic linear stretch $\lambda = (1/v_2)^{1/3}$. The elastic free energy per chain is

$$\frac{\Delta G_{elastic}}{N_2 RT} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\lambda^2 - 1\right) - 3 \ln \lambda$$

Differentiating and converting to osmotic form using $\pi_{elastic} = -\partial(\Delta G_{elastic})/\partial V$:

$$\pi_{elastic} = -RT\,\frac{v_e}{V_0}\left[v_2^{\,1/3} - \frac{v_2}{2}\right]$$

**(1 mark)** *Combining the two contributions and converting $v_e/V_0 = \rho_p/(M_c V_1)$.* Setting $\pi_{mix} + \pi_{elastic} = 0$:

$$\ln(1 - v_{2s}) + v_{2s} + \chi v_{2s}^{\,2} + \frac{\rho_p V_1}{M_c}\left(v_{2s}^{\,1/3} - \frac{v_{2s}}{2}\right) = 0$$

**(1 mark)** *Define every symbol:*
- $v_{2s}$ = equilibrium polymer volume fraction in the swollen gel,
- $\chi$ = Flory–Huggins polymer–solvent interaction parameter,
- $\rho_p$ = density of dry polymer ($\text{g/cm}^3$),
- $V_1$ = molar volume of the swelling solvent ($\text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$),
- $M_c$ = average molecular weight between crosslinks ($\text{g/mol}$).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**(i) Degree of swelling $Q$ [1 mark]:**

$$Q = \frac{m_s - m_d}{m_d} = \frac{3.85 - 0.250}{0.250} = \frac{3.60}{0.250} = 14.4$$

**(ii) Equilibrium polymer volume fraction $v_{2s}$ [2 marks]:**

$$v_{2s} = \frac{1}{1 + (\rho_p / \rho_s) \cdot Q} = \frac{1}{1 + (1.30 / 1.00) \cdot 14.4} = \frac{1}{1 + 18.72} = \frac{1}{19.72}$$

$$v_{2s} \approx 0.0507$$

**(iii) Average molecular weight between crosslinks $M_c$ [4 marks]:**

Rearrange the Flory–Rehner equation to isolate $M_c$:

$$M_c = -\frac{\rho_p V_1 \left(v_{2s}^{\,1/3} - v_{2s}/2\right)}{\ln(1 - v_{2s}) + v_{2s} + \chi v_{2s}^{\,2}}$$

**Compute the numerator:**

$$
v_{2s}^{1/3} = (0.0507)^{1/3} \approx 0.3686
$$

$$
v_{2s}/2 = 0.0254
$$

$$
v_{2s}^{1/3} - v_{2s}/2 = 0.3686 - 0.0254 = 0.3432
$$

$$
\text{Numerator} = -1.30 \cdot 18.04 \cdot 0.3432 \approx -8.05
$$

**Compute the denominator:**

$$
\ln(1 - v_{2s}) = \ln(0.9493) \approx -0.0520
$$

$$
v_{2s} = 0.0507
$$

$$
\chi v_{2s}^{\,2} = 0.48 \cdot (0.0507)^2 = 0.48 \cdot 0.00257 \approx 0.00123
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = -0.0520 + 0.0507 + 0.00123 = -0.00007 \approx -6.9 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Final $M_c$:**

$$
M_c = \frac{-8.05}{-6.9 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.17 \times 10^{5}~\text{g/mol}
$$

$$\boxed{M_c \approx 1.17 \times 10^{5}~\text{g/mol} = 117~\text{kg/mol}}$$

This large $M_c$ is consistent with a loosely crosslinked, highly swellable polyacrylamide hydrogel.

---

### Question B — Choice B: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Starting from the differential equation $dQ_t/dt = k(Q_{eq} - Q_t)^2$, derive the second-order swelling kinetic model of Schott in the linear form $t/Q_t = A + Bt$, and explain how $Q_{eq}$ and the rate constant $k$ are obtained experimentally.

**(b) [7 marks]** A crosslinked PVA hydrogel is dried and immersed in water at 30 °C. The swelling data recorded are: at $t = 10$ min, $Q = 1.20$; at $t = 20$ min, $Q = 1.95$; at $t = 30$ min, $Q = 2.45$; at $t = 40$ min, $Q = 2.78$; at $t = 50$ min, $Q = 3.00$. Using Schott's second-order model, determine the equilibrium swelling $Q_{eq}$ and the second-order rate constant $k$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Choice B)

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 marks]:**

**(1 mark)** State the starting differential equation:

$$\frac{dQ_t}{dt} = k (Q_{eq} - Q_t)^2$$

**(1 mark)** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dQ_t}{(Q_{eq} - Q_t)^2} = k \, dt$$

**(1 mark)** Integrate from $0$ to $Q_t$ and $0$ to $t$, using the standard integral $\int dx/(a-x)^2 = 1/(a-x)$:

$$\left[\frac{1}{Q_{eq} - Q_t}\right]_0^{Q_t} = k t$$

$$\frac{1}{Q_{eq} - Q_t} - \frac{1}{Q_{eq}} = k t$$

**(2 marks)** Algebraically rearrange to the linear form:

$$
\frac{1}{Q_{eq} - Q_t} = \frac{1}{Q_{eq}} + k t
$$

$$
\frac{Q_{eq}}{Q_{eq} - Q_t} = 1 + k Q_{eq} t
$$

$$
Q_{eq} - Q_t = \frac{Q_{eq}}{1 + k Q_{eq} t}
$$

$$
Q_t = \frac{k Q_{eq}^{\,2} t}{1 + k Q_{eq} t}
$$

$$
\frac{t}{Q_t} = \frac{1}{k Q_{eq}^{\,2}} + \frac{t}{Q_{eq}}
$$

**(2 marks)** Identify the linear form $t/Q_t = A + Bt$ with $A = 1/(k Q_{eq}^2)$ (intercept) and $B = 1/Q_{eq}$ (slope). The constants are obtained by plotting $t/Q_t$ against $t$ and performing a least-squares linear fit; $Q_{eq} = 1/B$ and $k = 1/(A \, Q_{eq}^2)$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate $t$, $Q_t$, and $t/Q_t$ [3 marks]:**

| $t$ (min) | $Q_t$ | $t / Q_t$ (min) |
| ---: | ---: | ---: |
| 10 | 1.20 | 8.333 |
| 20 | 1.95 | 10.256 |
| 30 | 2.45 | 12.245 |
| 40 | 2.78 | 14.388 |
| 50 | 3.00 | 16.667 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression of $t/Q_t$ against $t$ [2 marks]:**

Using the least-squares slope formula on the five data points:

$$
\text{slope } B = \frac{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum (t_i - \bar{t})^2}
$$

With $\bar{t} = 30$ and $\bar{y} = 12.378$, a numerical fit yields:

$$
B \approx 0.2083~\text{min}^{-1}, \quad A \approx 6.18~\text{min}\cdot\text{(g/g)}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Q_{eq}$ and $k$ [2 marks]:**

$$
Q_{eq} = \frac{1}{B} = \frac{1}{0.2083} \approx 4.80
$$

$$
k = \frac{1}{A \cdot Q_{eq}^{\,2}} = \frac{1}{6.18 \times (4.80)^2} = \frac{1}{6.18 \times 23.04} = \frac{1}{142.4}
$$

$$\boxed{Q_{eq} \approx 4.80~\text{g/g}, \quad k \approx 7.0 \times 10^{-3}~\text{g gel / (g solvent · min)}}$$

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks in swelling questions — and how to avoid each pitfall:**
> 1. **Mixing up $Q$ and $q$ (1 mark penalty).** A surprising number of answers use $q = m_s/m_d$ but then plug it into the Flory–Rehner equation expecting a $v_2$ value. The Flory–Rehner equation requires the polymer *volume fraction* $v_{2s}$, not the mass ratio. Convert: $v_{2s} = 1 / [1 + (\rho_p/\rho_s) Q]$.
> 2. **Forgetting the density ratio in the $Q \to v_2$ conversion (1 mark penalty).** If the numerical gives $\rho_p = 1.30$ and $\rho_s = 1.00$, you **must** use the ratio; setting it to unity is a common error.
> 3. **Sign error in the Flory–Rehner numerator (up to 2 marks lost).** When isolating $M_c$, the term $(v_{2s}^{1/3} - v_{2s}/2)$ is **positive** (for $v_{2s} < 0.4$), the logarithmic bracket $(-\ln(1-v_{2s}) + v_{2s} + \chi v_{2s}^2)$ is **negative** for $v_{2s} < 1$. The two negatives cancel, giving a positive $M_c$. Carrying the wrong sign yields a negative $M_c$ and full credit loss.
> 4. **Using the affine form for a phantom network (1 mark penalty).** If the question explicitly says "phantom network", replace $(v_{2s}^{1/3} - v_{2s}/2)$ with $(1/2 - v_{2s}/2)$.
> 5. **Kinetics — tabulating only $t$ vs $Q_t$ (1 mark penalty).** The Schott model demands a *third* column: $t/Q_t$. Examiners explicitly look for this table.
> 6. **Stating $\chi < 0.5$ for a poor solvent (conceptual, 1 mark).** $\chi$ is **inversely** related to solvent quality: $\chi < 0.5$ ⇒ *good* solvent; $\chi > 0.5$ ⇒ *poor* solvent.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Swelling equilibrium** is set by the **balance of osmotic (mixing) pressure and elastic retractive pressure** — never by either alone.
- **Three quantitative measures** of swelling: $Q = (m_s - m_d)/m_d$, $q = m_s/m_d$, $\text{EWC} = (m_s - m_d)/m_s$. Know when to use which.
- The **Flory–Rehner equation** is the *single most important equation* in this module — memorise both the affine and the phantom forms.
- **$M_c$** (molecular weight between crosslinks) decreases as crosslink density increases; high $M_c$ ⇒ highly swellable, soft, loosely crosslinked gel.
- **$\chi$ is dimensionless** and the **Hildebrand estimate** $\chi \approx 0.341 + (V_1/RT)(\delta_p - \delta_s)^2$ is useful when no direct $\chi$ data is given.
- **Conversion formulae** between gravimetric $Q$ and volumetric $v_{2s}$ must use the density ratio $\rho_p/\rho_s$ — never set it to 1 unless told to.
- **Mesh size $\xi$** controls drug release kinetics from hydrogels; $\xi$ scales as $v_{2s}^{-1/3} \cdot (M_c)^{1/2}$.
- **Schott's second-order kinetics** is the *most commonly tested* kinetic model in KTU; it always requires the $t/Q_t$ vs $t$ linearisation.
- **Korsmeyer–Peppas power-law exponent $n$** classifies diffusion: $n = 0.5$ ⇒ Fickian; $0.5 < n < 1$ ⇒ non-Fickian/anomalous; $n = 1$ ⇒ Case II transport.
- **Ionic gels** (polyelectrolytes) gain an additional osmotic contribution $\pi_{ion}$ from the mobile counter-ions — Donnan equilibrium applies. KTU may ask a 7-mark sub-question on this.
- **Swelling is a probe of network structure**: from a single $Q$ measurement, combined with $\chi$ from an independent swelling/viscosity experiment, you can extract $M_c$, $v_e$, and $\xi$ — these are the three key structural descriptors of a gel.
- **Stability requirement for any real gel**: the crosslink density must be **high enough** to prevent dissolution but **low enough** to permit useful swelling — this is the design trade-off every KTU design question will probe.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Properties and Its Characterisation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT525 - POLYMER ELECTROLYTES & GELS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties and Its Characterisation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4: Mechanical Properties and Its Characterisation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **polymer electrolytes and gels**, *mechanical properties* refer to the suite of measurable responses of these soft-solid hybrid materials when subjected to external mechanical stimuli — namely, force, deformation, or oscillatory stress. The characteristic mechanical descriptors include **Young's modulus (E)**, **tensile strength (σ_b)**, **elongation at break (ε_b)**, **storage modulus (E')**, **loss modulus (E'')**, **loss tangent (tan δ)**, **hardness**, **toughness**, and the **viscoelastic** parameters (creep compliance, stress relaxation modulus). For a KTU 2024 Scheme assessment, these properties are typically interpreted through the lens of *viscoelasticity theory* because polymer electrolytes/gels simultaneously store and dissipate energy, behaving neither as a pure elastic solid (Hookean) nor as a pure viscous liquid (Newtonian).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT525, Module 4):** The mechanical integrity of a polymer electrolyte film determines whether it survives the rigours of battery/fuel-cell fabrication (electrode lamination, winding) and long-term cycling. A "good" ionic conductor that crumbles under stress is engineering-failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kerala Halwa" Model

Imagine a freshly prepared **Kerala-style halwa** (a soft, jelly-like sweet) versus a piece of **hard coconut jaggery**:

- The **halwa** stretches when you pull it, slowly regains shape, and feels "rubbery." This is a polymer electrolyte/gel — it stores some energy elastically (the cross-linked polymer network) and dissipates some as heat through viscous flow of the plasticiser/solvent. We call this **viscoelasticity**.

- The **jaggery** is brittle: it stores little energy and snaps abruptly. This is a purely elastic, glassy polymer — not useful as a flexible electrolyte separator.

- If you add too much water to the halwa, it flows like a thick liquid (the polymer network is too dilute — gel becomes a *sol*). If you crosslink it too tightly, it becomes a tough rubber.

- The *mechanical property characterisation* is simply the kitchen test done scientifically: we apply a known force/displacement and *quantify* how the material responds.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Units in **bold**)

| Property | Symbol | Typical Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | $E$ | **MPa / GPa** | Stiffness under uniaxial tension |
| Tensile Strength | $\sigma_b$ | **MPa** | Maximum stress before rupture |
| Elongation at Break | $\varepsilon_b$ | **%** | Maximum strain before rupture |
| Storage Modulus | $E'$ | **MPa** | Elastic (stored) energy component |
| Loss Modulus | $E''$ | **MPa** | Viscous (dissipated) energy component |
| Loss Tangent | $\tan\delta$ | **dimensionless** | $E''/E'$ — damping ratio |
| Glass Transition Temp | $T_g$ | **°C / K** | Onset of segmental mobility |
| Crosslink Density | $\nu_e$ | **mol/m³** | Number of elastically active chains per unit volume |
| Creep Compliance | $J(t)$ | **Pa⁻¹** | Time-dependent strain under constant stress |
| Relaxation Modulus | $G(t)$ | **Pa** | Time-dependent stress under constant strain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Kerala's R&D ecosystem:** With ISRO, CMET, and several lithium-ion startups (ION Energy, Gegadyne) in India working on solid-state batteries, the *simultaneous* optimisation of ionic conductivity (>10⁻⁴ S/cm) AND mechanical strength (>1 MPa tensile) is the central engineering challenge — and the topic of this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Viscoelastic Paradigm

A polymer electrolyte is a *thermorheologically complex* material. Its mechanical response is intermediate between an **ideal Hookean solid** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$, energy stored fully) and an **ideal Newtonian fluid** ($\sigma = \eta \dot{\varepsilon}$, energy dissipated fully). The combined response is called **viscoelasticity**, and it is the conceptual anchor of this entire module.

### 2.2 Linear Viscoelasticity — The Two Compliance Regimes

#### A. Creep (constant stress, time-dependent strain)
A constant stress $\sigma_0$ is applied, and strain $\varepsilon(t)$ is recorded. The response is quantified by **creep compliance** $J(t)$:

$$J(t) = \frac{\varepsilon(t)}{\sigma_0}$$

For a polymer electrolyte, $J(t)$ has three distinct regions:
1. **Instantaneous elastic** ($J_0$) — bond stretching
2. **Retarded viscoelastic** ($J_R(t)$) — uncoiling of polymer chains, governed by Kelvin–Voigt elements
3. **Newtonian viscous flow** ($t/\eta_0$) — long-term flow of the matrix

The total compliance (Burgers model) is:

$$J(t) = J_0 + \sum_{i} J_i \left[1 - \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_i}\right)\right] + \frac{t}{\eta_0}$$

where $\tau_i = \eta_i J_i$ is the retardation time of the $i$-th Kelvin–Voigt element.

#### B. Stress Relaxation (constant strain, time-dependent stress)
A constant strain $\varepsilon_0$ is imposed, and stress $\sigma(t)$ decays. The **relaxation modulus** $G(t)$ is:

$$G(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$$

For a polymer electrolyte, $G(t)$ often follows a stretched-exponential (Kohlrausch–Williams–Watts, KWW) form:

$$G(t) = G_\infty + (G_0 - G_\infty) \exp\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\tau}\right)^\beta\right]$$

where $0 < \beta \le 1$ is the stretching parameter (heterogeneity indicator), $\tau$ is the characteristic relaxation time, and $G_0, G_\infty$ are the instantaneous and equilibrium moduli.

### 2.3 Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA) — The Heart of Characterisation

In DMA, a sinusoidal stress $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied and the resulting strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$ lags by a phase angle $\delta$. This is the most common KTU-lab and KTU-exam question format.

#### The Complex Modulus

$$E^* = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon(t)} = E' + iE''$$

Storage modulus (in-phase, elastic):

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} \cos\delta$$

Loss modulus (out-of-phase, viscous):

$$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} \sin\delta$$

Loss tangent (damping factor):

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation for a gel/polymer electrolyte:**
> - At $T \ll T_g$: $E' \gg E''$, $\tan\delta \ll 1$ → glassy, brittle, ion-conduction frozen.
> - At $T = T_g$: $E''$ peaks (α-relaxation), $\tan\delta$ peaks → onset of segmental motion (the same motion that enables ionic hopping! This is why $T_g$ and ionic conductivity are correlated — the *Walden–Angell* connection).
> - At $T \gg T_g$: $E'$ plateaus (rubbery plateau if crosslinked) → ideal operating window for a flexible electrolyte.

### 2.4 Rubber Elasticity Theory (for Crosslinked Gels)

For a covalently crosslinked polymer gel (e.g., PEO–PEGDA network), the **equilibrium shear modulus** in the rubbery regime follows the classical theory of rubber elasticity:

$$G = \nu_e R T = \frac{\rho R T}{M_c}$$

where:
- $\nu_e$ = number density of elastically active network chains (mol/m³)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature
- $\rho$ = density of the polymer
- $M_c$ = molecular weight between crosslinks

For small deformations, $E \approx 3G$ (incompressible assumption, Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.5$).

### 2.5 Stress–Strain Behaviour — The Universal Curve

For a ductile polymer electrolyte film under uniaxial tension, the engineering stress–strain ($\sigma$–$\varepsilon$) curve passes through four regimes:
1. **Linear elastic** (Hookean, slope = $E$)
2. **Yield** (onset of plastic deformation at $\sigma_y$)
3. **Strain hardening** (molecular orientation)
4. **Fracture** (catastrophic failure at $\sigma_b$, $\varepsilon_b$)

**Toughness** $U_T$ (energy to break per unit volume) = area under the $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve:

$$U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma(\varepsilon)\, d\varepsilon$$

### 2.6 Time–Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)

Polymer electrolytes are *thermo-rheologically simple* in many practical cases. The principle states: **a change in temperature is equivalent to a horizontal shift of the modulus curve on a logarithmic time/frequency axis**, with a shift factor $a_T$ given empirically by the **Williams–Landel–Ferry (WLF) equation**:

$$\log_{10} a_T = -\frac{C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$$

For $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$, the universal constants are $C_1 \approx 17.44$ and $C_2 \approx 51.6$ K (above $T_g$). Below $T_g$, the **Arrhenius equation** is more appropriate:

$$a_T = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{\text{ref}}}\right)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** TTSP lets engineers construct *master curves* of $E'$ and $E''$ spanning 10–15 decades of frequency from DMA data collected over a narrow temperature window (e.g., −80 °C to +100 °C). This is critical for predicting long-term creep (years) of battery separators from short-term lab tests (hours).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^* = E' + iE''$ | Complex modulus (DMA) | Pa |
| 2 | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | Loss tangent (damping) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's law (linear elastic) | Pa |
| 4 | $E(t) = E_\infty + (E_0 - E_\infty)\exp[-(t/\tau)^\beta]$ | Stress relaxation (KWW) | Pa |
| 5 | $J(t) = J_0 + \sum_i J_i[1-\exp(-t/\tau_i)] + t/\eta_0$ | Creep compliance (Burgers) | Pa⁻¹ |
| 6 | $G = \nu_e RT = \rho RT/M_c$ | Rubber elasticity modulus | Pa |
| 7 | $E \approx 3G$ | Incompressible rubber | Pa |
| 8 | $U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma\, d\varepsilon$ | Toughness (energy density) | J/m³ |
| 9 | $\log_{10} a_T = -C_1(T-T_r)/[C_2+(T-T_r)]$ | WLF shift factor | dimensionless |
| 10 | $a_T = \exp[(E_a/R)(1/T - 1/T_r)]$ | Arrhenius shift (below $T_g$) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $E''_{max}$ at $T_g$ → $\alpha$-relaxation | DMA peak identification | K / °C |
| 12 | $\eta^* = E^*/(i\omega)$ | Complex viscosity | Pa·s |
| 13 | $H = P/(2.828 L)$ (Brinell) | Hardness (empirical) | — |
| 14 | $D \sim \exp(-E_a/k_BT)$ | Diffusivity (VTF/Nernst–Einstein) | m²/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU board paper:** Of these 14 equations, equations **1–3, 6, 7, 9, 11** are the *highest-yield* — they appear in nearly every Part B question on mechanical properties. The KTU 2024 Scheme gives weightage to *derivation* style questions on the WLF equation and rubber elasticity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: From Sinusoidal Stress/Strain to Complex Modulus

We start with the KTU-classical problem: a polymer electrolyte is subjected to a sinusoidal stress at angular frequency $\omega$. Show that the complex modulus separates into storage and loss components, and derive $\tan\delta$.

### Step 1 — Write the imposed stress

The dynamic mechanical analyser applies a harmonic tensile stress:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

where $\sigma_0$ is the stress amplitude (Pa) and $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency (rad/s).

### Step 2 — Write the resulting strain (with phase lag)

For a viscoelastic material, the strain response is sinusoidal but lags the stress by a phase angle $\delta$ ($0 \le \delta \le \pi/2$):

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t - \delta)$$

This lag is the *signature* of energy dissipation — purely elastic solids have $\delta = 0$, purely viscous liquids have $\delta = \pi/2$.

### Step 3 — Express stress and strain as complex exponentials

Using Euler's identity ($\sin x = \text{Im}[e^{ix}]$), we move to the complex plane for algebraic convenience:

$$\sigma^*(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(i\omega t)$$

$$\varepsilon^*(t) = \varepsilon_0 \exp[i(\omega t - \delta)] = \varepsilon_0 e^{-i\delta} \exp(i\omega t)$$

The $*$ denotes the complex representation (NOT complex conjugate in the strict sense — it is a phasor).

### Step 4 — Define the complex modulus

By direct analogy with Hooke's law in the frequency domain:

$$E^*(\omega) = \frac{\sigma^*(t)}{\varepsilon^*(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0 e^{-i\delta}} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} e^{i\delta}$$

### Step 5 — Separate real and imaginary parts

Using $e^{i\delta} = \cos\delta + i\sin\delta$:

$$E^*(\omega) = \left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta\right) + i\left(\frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta\right)$$

### Step 6 — Identify storage and loss moduli

By definition:

$$E' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\cos\delta \quad \text{(in-phase, elastic energy storage)}$$

$$E'' = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0}\sin\delta \quad \text{(out-of-phase, viscous energy dissipation)}$$

### Step 7 — Derive the loss tangent

The ratio of imaginary to real parts:

$$\tan\delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\delta = \frac{\sin\delta}{\cos\delta} = \frac{E''(\omega)}{E'(\omega)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU board-marking interpretation:** The above is the canonical 7-line derivation that KTU examiners expect in Part B questions on DMA. Missing the explicit use of Euler's identity, or confusing the phase lag direction, costs up to **2 marks**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: Equilibrium Modulus of a Crosslinked Polymer Gel (Rubber Elasticity)

### Step 1 — Consider a crosslinked polymer network

A polymer gel (e.g., PEO–PMMA with PEGDA crosslinker) consists of $N$ network chains of end-to-end vector $\vec{r}$ in a volume $V$. The number density of chains is $\nu = N/V$.

### Step 2 — Entropic origin of elasticity

The elastic force in a rubbery network is *entropic* (not enthalpic). The classical Helmholtz free energy of a single Gaussian chain stretched to end-to-end distance $r$ is:

$$F_{\text{chain}} = \frac{3k_B T r^2}{2 b^2} + F_0(T)$$

where $b$ is the Kuhn segment length and $F_0$ is the temperature-dependent reference free energy (contributes to thermal expansion, not to the mechanical modulus).

### Step 3 — Total free energy of the network (affine network model)

For an affine network (junction points move proportionally with macroscopic strain), the total free energy density is:

$$\frac{\mathcal{F}}{V} = \frac{1}{2} \nu_e k_B T \left(\lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 - 3\right)$$

where $\lambda_x, \lambda_y, \lambda_z$ are the principal stretch ratios.

### Step 4 — Apply uniaxial extension

In uniaxial extension along $x$ by stretch ratio $\lambda$, with volume conservation ($\lambda_y = \lambda_z = 1/\sqrt{\lambda}$):

$$\lambda_x^2 + \lambda_y^2 + \lambda_z^2 = \lambda^2 + \frac{2}{\lambda}$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate to obtain the nominal (engineering) stress

The nominal stress is $\sigma = \partial(\mathcal{F}/V)/\partial\lambda$:

$$\sigma = \nu_e k_B T \left(\lambda - \frac{1}{\lambda^2}\right)$$

### Step 6 — Take the small-strain limit

For $\lambda \to 1$, expand using $\lambda \approx 1 + \varepsilon$ and $1/\lambda^2 \approx 1 - 2\varepsilon$:

$$\sigma = \nu_e k_B T \left[(1+\varepsilon) - (1-2\varepsilon)\right] = 3\nu_e k_B T\, \varepsilon$$

### Step 7 — Identify the Young's modulus

Since $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$:

$$E = 3\nu_e k_B T = 3\nu_e R T \quad \text{(per mole)}$$

Using $\nu_e = \rho/M_c$ (chains per unit volume from polymer density $\rho$ and inter-crosslink molecular weight $M_c$):

$$\boxed{E = \frac{3\rho R T}{M_c}}$$

This is the canonical result that the **modulus is inversely proportional to the inter-crosslink molecular weight** — a cornerstone of gel mechanics and a frequent KTU exam hit.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: WLF Equation from Free-Volume Theory (Sketch)

The WLF equation is empirical but can be rationalised via free-volume arguments:

### Step 1 — Free volume fraction

Define the fractional free volume $f = (V - V_0)/V_0$, where $V_0$ is the occupied volume.

### Step 2 — Doolittle equation for viscosity

$$\ln \eta = \ln A + \frac{B}{f}$$

### Step 3 — Linear temperature dependence of free volume

$$f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

where $f_g$ is the free volume at $T_g$ and $\alpha_f$ is the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

### Step 4 — Combine

The ratio of viscosities at two temperatures is the shift factor:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \log_{10}\left[\frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}\right] = \frac{1}{2.303}\left[\frac{B}{f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)} - \frac{B}{f_g}\right]$$

### Step 5 — Identify the universal constants

With $B = 1$ and $f_g = 0.025$ (Doolittle's universal values), $\alpha_f = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303 f_g} = 17.44, \quad C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} = 51.6 \text{ K}$$

This yields the WLF equation in its standard form quoted in §2.6.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students often quote $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ without the reference temperature $T_r = T_g$. Always state the reference — losing 1 mark for missing it is a common KTU pitfall.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Composition on Mechanical Properties

| System Modification | Effect on $E'$ / $E$ | Effect on $\sigma_b$ | Effect on $\varepsilon_b$ | Effect on $T_g$ | Effect on $\sigma$ (ionic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Crosslink density ($\nu_e$) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑ Slightly | ↓↓ Strongly | ↑ Slightly | ↓ (restricts segmental motion) |
| ↑ Plasticiser content (e.g., EC, PC) | ↓↓ Strongly | ↓ (often) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↓↓ Strongly | ↑↑ (more free volume) |
| ↑ Salt concentration (e.g., LiTFSI) | ↑ Initially, then ↓ | Variable | ↓ | ↑ (ion-dipole coupling) | ↑ to optimum, then ↓ |
| ↑ Filler (e.g., SiO₂, Al₂O₃ nano) | ↑↑ Strongly | ↑↑ | ↓ | Often ↑ | ↑ (percolation at interface) |
| ↑ Temperature (above $T_g$) | ↓↓ (rubbery plateau if crosslinked) | ↓ | ↑ | — | ↑ (VTF) |
| ↑ Frequency of DMA | ↑ | — | — | Shift of $T_g$ peak | — |

---

## 4.1 DMA Measurement — Block Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *functional architecture* of a Dynamic Mechanical Analyser as it interrogates a polymer electrolyte/gel film, since the actual instrument cross-section is too complex to render directly.
