## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a polymer gel electrolyte. List any three advantages of gel polymer electrolytes over liquid electrolytes in lithium-ion batteries.**

**Model Answer (Board-Exam Quality):**

> **Definition:** A polymer gel electrolyte is a quasi-solid ionic conductor in which a liquid electrolyte (salt + solvent) is immobilized within a three-dimensional polymer matrix, combining the high ionic conductivity of liquids with the mechanical stability of solids.
>
> **Three advantages:**
> 1. **Leak-proof operation** — the polymer network prevents solvent leakage, enabling flexible and miniaturized device designs.
> 2. **Suppressed dendrite growth** — the semi-rigid matrix physically blocks lithium dendrite penetration, improving safety in Li-metal batteries.
> 3. **Wide electrochemical stability window (> 4.5 V)** — enables use with high-voltage cathodes (NMC811, LNMO).
> 4. *(Bonus)* Better thermal stability and reduced flammability.

**Marks split (KTU pattern):** Definition 1 M + 3 advantages × 1/2 M + Conclusion 1/2 M = 3 M.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Arrhenius and VFT behaviour in polymer gel electrolytes. When is each applicable?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Arrhenius | VFT (Vogel–Fulcher–Tammann) |
|---------|-----------|------------------------------|
| Equation | $\sigma T = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_a/k_B T)$ | $\sigma = A T^{-1/2} \exp[-B/(T-T_0)]$ |
| Mechanism | Simple ion-hopping over a fixed barrier | Coupled ion transport with polymer segmental motion |
| Applicable to | Crystalline / dry / low-plasticizer gels | Amorphous / highly plasticized gels |
| Plot | $\ln(\sigma T)$ vs $1/T$ is **linear** | $\ln(\sigma T^{1/2})$ vs $1/(T-T_0)$ is **linear** |
| Reference temp. | None | $T_0$ (Vogel temperature, ~ 30–50 K below $T_g$) |

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Discuss the structural requirements of a polymer matrix for use as a gel electrolyte in a Li-ion battery. Explain the role of plasticizers and cross-linkers with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Bruce–Vincent equation for the lithium-ion transference number in a polymer gel electrolyte. Explain the significance of $t_+ \to 1$ in single-ion conducting gels. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (a):

**Structural requirements of the polymer matrix:**

1. **Low glass transition temperature ($T_g$):** Should be well below room temperature (e.g., PEO $T_g \approx -67\ °C$) so that segmental motion is active at operating temperature.
2. **Polar backbone with electron-donating atoms:** Ether (-O-), carbonyl (-C=O), or nitrile (-C≡N) groups coordinate Li⁺ and facilitate hopping. *Example:* PEO, PVdF-HFP, PAN.
3. **Ability to form a 3D network via cross-linking:** Provides mechanical strength. *Cross-linkers:* Poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate, glutaraldehyde, divinylbenzene.
4. **Electrochemical inertness:** Stable up to > 4.5 V vs Li/Li⁺.
5. **Film-forming ability:** Free-standing, flexible film 25–200 µm thick.

**Role of plasticizers:**

- Plasticizers (EC, PC, DMC, PEG) reduce crystallinity and $T_g$, increasing free volume.
- They boost ionic conductivity by **3–4 orders of magnitude** (from ~10⁻⁷ to ~10⁻³ S/cm).
- *Example:* PEO + 30 wt% EC gives σ ≈ 10⁻⁴ S/cm at 25 °C.

**Role of cross-linkers:**

- Cross-linkers (e.g., PEGDA, MBA) create covalent bridges between polymer chains.
- They prevent the polymer from **dissolving** in the liquid plasticizer.
- A delicate balance: too few cross-links → dissolves; too many → brittle, low conductivity.
- **Optimum cross-link density:** 1 crosslink per 50–200 monomers.

**Diagram (textual):**

Polymer chain A —— Crosslinker —— Polymer chain B
        |                            |
        O — CH₂ — CH₂ — O            (PEO backbone, with ether O coordinating Li⁺)

**Conclusion:** An ideal polymer matrix must balance **conductivity (low Tg, amorphous, polar)** with **mechanical integrity (cross-linked network)**, achievable through careful selection of host polymer, plasticizer, and cross-linker.

> **Marks split (KTU valuation):** 5 structural reqs × 0.5 M = 2.5 M; Plasticizer role 2 M; Cross-linker role 2 M; Example 0.5 M.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A (b):

**Derivation of the Bruce–Vincent equation:**

Consider a symmetric cell: **Li metal | GPE | Li metal**.

Step 1: Apply a small constant DC potential $\Delta V$ across the cell.

Step 2: The **initial current** $I_0$ is determined by the bulk resistance of the gel:

$$
I_0 = \frac{\Delta V}{R_0}
$$

Step 3: As Li⁺ is **consumed at the cathode** (plating) and **depleted at the anode** (stripping), concentration gradients build up, increasing the cell resistance.

Step 4: After sufficient time, the system reaches a **steady state** where Li⁺ flux equals the flux of salt anions. The current becomes constant:

$$
I_{ss} = \frac{\Delta V}{R_{ss}}
$$

Step 5: The lithium transference number $t_+$ is defined as the fraction of total current carried by Li⁺:

$$
t_+ = \frac{J_{Li^+}}{J_{Li^+} + J_{X^-}}
$$

Combining the equations:

$$
t_+ = \frac{I_{ss} (\Delta V - I_0 R_0)}{I_0 (\Delta V - I_{ss} R_{ss})}
$$

For thin gels where $R_0 \approx R_{ss}$:

$$
\boxed{t_+ \approx \frac{I_{ss}}{I_0}}
$$

**Significance of $t_+ \to 1$ (single-ion conductors):**

- In a **bi-ionic gel**, anions ($TFSI^-$, $PF_6^-$) also migrate, causing **concentration polarization** that limits power density and causes voltage decay.
- In a **single-ion gel** (e.g., PEO + lithium poly(styrene sulfonate) or PEO + Li-polyacrylate), the anion is **covalently tethered** to the polymer backbone and is immobile.
- Hence **only Li⁺ moves** → $t_+ \to 1$ → no concentration polarization → stable cycling at high current densities → enables Li-metal anodes without dendrite problems.

> **KTU Board Tip:** Single-ion conductors achieve $t_+ = 0.85 - 1.0$, vs $0.3 - 0.5$ for conventional gels. This is a major research focus in KTU M.Tech project reports.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loses 1–2 Marks):**
> Many students write the **Bruce–Vincent equation** without explaining the *physical meaning* of $I_0$ and $I_{ss}$ or the *limitation* ($R_0 \approx R_{ss}$). The examiner will deduct 1 M if you simply quote the formula without derivation. Always show the **5 steps above**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare hydrogel, organogel, and ionogel electrolytes. Discuss their respective advantages and limitations for application in (i) Zn-air batteries and (ii) Li-metal batteries. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of the Nernst–Einstein relation, derive the expression for ionic conductivity of a binary salt dissociated in a polymer gel. Discuss the role of ion pairing in reducing the effective conductivity. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (a):

**Comparative Table:**

| Property | Hydrogel | Organogel | Ionogel |
|----------|----------|-----------|---------|
| Liquid phase | Aqueous (H₂O) | Organic carbonates | Ionic liquids (ILs) |
| Conductivity | 10⁻³ – 10⁻¹ S/cm | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ S/cm | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻³ S/cm |
| ESW | ~1.23 V (water splitting) | 4.5 – 5.5 V | 4.5 – 6.0 V |
| Flammability | None (water) | High (organic) | None (non-volatile) |
| Temperature range | 0 – 80 °C | −20 – 60 °C | −40 – 250 °C |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |

**Application (i) — Zn-air batteries:**

- **Best choice: Hydrogel** (e.g., PVA + KOH, gelatin + KOH).
- *Why:* The aqueous phase provides high $\sigma$ (10⁻¹ S/cm) needed for the alkaline ORR/OER reaction.
- *Hydrogel limitation:* ESW < 1.5 V limits energy density.

**Application (ii) — Li-metal batteries:**

- **Best choice: Ionogel** (e.g., PVdF-HFP + EMImTFSI + LiTFSI).
- *Why:* High ESW (> 5 V) accommodates high-voltage cathodes; non-flammable IL prevents thermal runaway; wide temperature range suits EV operation.
- *Organogel alternative:* PVdF-HFP + EC/DMC + LiPF₆ is the **industry standard** for Li-ion pouch cells (Sony, LG, Samsung), but is flammable.
- *Hydrogel unsuitable:* Reacts violently with Li metal.

**Conclusion:** The choice of gel type is dictated by the **electrochemical stability window** and **anode compatibility** of the target battery chemistry.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B (b):

**Derivation of Nernst–Einstein conductivity for a binary salt:**

Step 1: Consider a salt $LiX$ dissociating in the gel:

$$
LiX \rightleftharpoons Li^+ + X^-
$$

Step 2: For each ion species $i$, Einstein's relation links mobility and diffusivity:

$$
\mu_i = \frac{q_i D_i}{k_B T}
$$

Step 3: The conductivity due to species $i$ is:

$$
\sigma_i = n_i q_i \mu_i = \frac{n_i q_i^2 D_i}{k_B T}
$$

Step 4: For a binary 1:1 salt, total conductivity is the sum of cation and anion contributions:

$$
\sigma = \sigma_+ + \sigma_- = \frac{n q^2 D_+}{k_B T} + \frac{n q^2 D_-}{k_B T}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma = \frac{n q^2 (D_+ + D_-)}{k_B T}}
$$

where $n$ = number density of dissociated salt pairs, $q$ = elementary charge.

**Role of ion pairing (non-ideality correction):**

In concentrated gels (n > 0.5 M), the assumption of **complete dissociation** breaks down. A fraction $\alpha$ of ions exist as **neutral ion pairs** (contact ion pairs, CIP) or **solvent-shared ion pairs** (SSIP), which do **not contribute to conductivity**.

The effective concentration of conducting ions is:

$$
n_{eff} = \alpha n
$$

A more realistic conductivity expression uses the **Haven ratio** $H_R$:

$$
\sigma_{real} = H_R \cdot \frac{n_{eff} q^2 (D_+ + D_-)}{k_B T}
$$

with $H_R \leq 1$ (typically 0.5–0.9 in concentrated gel electrolytes).

**Engineering implication:** Adding low-dielectric-constant solvents or large anions (TFSI⁻, FSI⁻) promotes ion pairing, **reducing σ**. Conversely, high-dielectric solvents (EC, $\varepsilon_r \approx 90$) suppress pairing and maximize σ.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write $\sigma = n q^2 D / k_B T$ **without specifying that it is the total over both ions**. Forgetting the $+$ and $-$ contributions loses 2 marks in Part (b).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 4)**

- ✅ **Definition:** Polymer gel electrolyte = polymer matrix + liquid electrolyte (immobilized) = quasi-solid ionic conductor.
- ✅ **Conductivity range:** $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-2}$ S/cm at 25 °C (target: $\geq 10^{-3}$ S/cm for practical use).
- ✅ **Best commercial matrix for Li-ion:** PVdF-HFP (because of low Tg, high dielectric constant, mechanical strength).
- ✅ **Three ion-transport mechanisms:** (1) Vehicle mechanism in solvent, (2) Hopping along polymer chains, (3) Segmental motion coupling.
- ✅ **Arrhenius** → crystalline gels; **VFT** → amorphous/plasticized gels.
- ✅ **Nernst–Einstein:** $\sigma = n q^2 (D_+ + D_-)/(k_B T)$.
- ✅ **Bruce–Vincent:** $t_+ = I_{ss}(\Delta V - I_0 R_0)/[I_0(\Delta V - I_{ss} R_{ss})]$.
- ✅ **Ion pairing** reduces $\sigma$ at high salt concentrations.
- ✅ **Single-ion conductors** have $t_+ \to 1$ — no concentration polarization, ideal for Li-metal.
- ✅ **Hydrogel** for Zn-air; **Organogel** for Li-ion; **Ionogel** for high-voltage/safe applications.
- ✅ **Energy density of supercap:** $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$; $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d$.
- ✅ **Key safety advantage:** Gel electrolytes suppress Li-dendrite growth due to mechanical rigidity of the polymer network.
- ✅ **Viva favourite:** "Why not use pure liquid electrolytes?" — Answer: leakage, flammability, dendrite growth, narrow ESW, packaging difficulty.
- ✅ **Viva favourite 2:** "What is the role of EC in PEO-based gel?" — Answer: EC acts as a high-dielectric plasticizer that dissociates LiPF₆ and reduces PEO crystallinity, boosting σ by ~1000×.
